items.question: Who uses gradient vectors in machine learning?
items.answer: Training pipelines, optimizers and autograd systems use ∇L to update billions of parameters; researchers also inspect input and activation gradients for debugging and interpretability.

items.question: What is the gradient of a scalar function f at x?
items.answer: The vector ∇f(x) = (∂f/∂x₁, …, ∂f/∂xₙ)ᵀ whose components are partial derivatives of f with respect to each input coordinate.

items.question: When is the gradient zero?
items.answer: At critical points of f—local maxima, minima or saddle points—where there is no first-order direction of increase or decrease (assuming differentiability).

items.question: Where does the gradient point relative to level sets?
items.answer: Orthogonal to level curves or surfaces of f at x; tangent motion along a level set has zero first-order change in f.

items.question: Why use −∇L instead of ∇L when training?
items.answer: We minimize loss L; −∇L is the direction of steepest local decrease, so stepping along it reduces L to first order.

items.question: How do you compute ∇f for f(x, y) = x² + xy?
items.answer: ∂f/∂x = 2x + y, ∂f/∂y = x, so ∇f(x, y) = (2x + y, x)ᵀ.

items.question: Gradient vs single-variable derivative: what changes in meaning?
items.answer: The 1D derivative is one slope along the x-axis. The gradient is a vector in input space: each component is a partial derivative, and together they pick the steepest ascent direction among all directions.

items.question: Gradient vs arbitrary partial derivative list: why bundle into ∇f?
items.answer: A flat list of partials lacks geometric direction. Assembling them into ∇f enables dot products with unit directions (directional derivatives), steepest-ascent interpretation and norm as max rate of change.
